summary:
The Gutian Dynasty of Sumer, approximately 2154–2112 BC, refers to a period of political dominance by the Gutians — a tribal people from the Zagros Mountains of what is now western Iran — over much of Mesopotamia following the [[event:1796|collapse of the Akkadian Empire]]. Ancient Mesopotamian sources, particularly the Sumerian King List, portray the Gutian period as a time of darkness and disorder, with a rapid succession of short-reigning kings whose control over the core Mesopotamian city-states was incomplete and contested.

The Gutian interlude is historically complex and difficult to assess impartially, since most surviving sources were composed by later Sumerian scribes hostile to these foreign rulers. While the Gutians likely disrupted trade networks and the centralised administration built by the Akkadian Empire, archaeological evidence from Sumerian cities during this period does not uniformly indicate sharp economic or cultural decline. Local rulers in cities such as Lagash maintained considerable autonomy and even experienced cultural flourishing — [[event:3733|Gudea of Lagash]], who governed during this era, was a prolific builder whose diorite statues are masterpieces of ancient art.

The Gutian period ended when Utu-hengal, king of Uruk, defeated the last Gutian king and reunited Mesopotamia, paving the way for Ur-Nammu to found [[event:1724|the Third Dynasty of Ur]]. The Gutian interval is significant in world history as an example of how peripheral tribal groups could disrupt and reconfigure imperial systems, and as a reminder that Mesopotamian history in this era was shaped not only by the great urban civilisations of the plains but also by the mobile peoples of the surrounding highlands.
